Off Calendar Apr-16-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. KRISTEN DRAKE, Esq. of Levin Simes LLP (tel: 415-426-3000) for Plaintiff.
Atty. LISA CORMAN, Esq. of Koeller, Nebeker, Carlson & Haluck, LLP (tel: 916-724-5700) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT FRANK LUCCHETTI INDIVIDUALLY AND D/B/A LUCCHETTI PROPERTIES' Motion To Compel Ulrich Berg M.D.'S Deposition And Production Of Documents Pursuant To Subpoenas.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling with modifications.

Defendant Frank Lucchetti's motion to compel production is denied for failure to file a meet and confer declaration. (See CCP 2025.480 ["(a) If a deponent fails to answer any question or to produce any document, electronically stored information, or tangible thing under the deponent's control that is specified in the deposition notice or a deposition subpoena, the party seeking discovery may move the court for an order compelling that answer or production. (b) This motion shall be made no later than 60 days after the completion of the record of the deposition, and shall be accompanied by a meet and confer declaration under section 2016.040"]; Bd. of Registered Nursing v. Superior Ct. of Orange Cnty. (2021) 59 Cal.App.5th 1011, 1031.) The Corman declaration states that the subpoena service issued letters to the nonparty subpoena target and that Defendant's counsel called the nonparty concerning the subpoena (Corman Dec. paras. 2, 5), but that discussion relates to an earlier subpoena whose time to move to compel has expired. No meet-and-confer efforts are recited with respect to the new subpoena as to which Defendant seeks to compel production.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL DEFENDANT'S DEPOSITION
Off Calendar Apr-15-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. KRISTEN DRAKE, Esq. of Levin Simes LLP (tel: 415-426-3000) for Plaintiff.
Atty. JESSE BOYD, Esq. of Koeller, Nebeker, Carlson & Haluck, LLP (tel: 916-724-5700) for Defendant.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO COMPEL DEFENDANT'S DEPOSITION.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as modified.

Plaintiff Nkechi Emeruwa-Neuberg moves to compel defendant Frank Lucchetti's deposition and production of documents. Emeruwa-Neuberg also seeks sanctions.

The motion is granted in part and denied in part. Lucchetti is ordered to sit for deposition in this matter within five court days of entry of this order if he has not already done so. Zoom is acceptable if Mr. Lucchetti provides a doctor's note concerning live testimony. Otherwise, the deposition will be in person. The court denies the motion to compel production of documents because responsive documents were previously produced and because Emeruwa-Neuberg has not set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the production. (CCP 2025.450(b)(1).) Emeruwa-Neuberg's reply briefing justifying her particular document requests comes too late.

The court denies the request for sanctions. It appears both parties have conditioned their witnesses' availability for deposition on the other party's conduct. That is a misuse of the discovery process and the court finds both parties' positions in that regard to lack substantial justification.

Counsel for the Plaintiff is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Kristen E. Drake for Nkechi Emeruwa-Neuberg, Plaintiff.
Lisa A. Corman for Frank Lucchetti, Defendant.

Ms. Corman contested the court's tentative decision. Ms. Corman raised objections to the Court's consideration of plaintiff's late-filed opposition, arguing that good cause had not been shown. Ms. Drake responded that the late filing resulted from an inadvertent application of the prior 14-day deadline under the former version of the statute. She stated the opposition was originally prepared under the assumption the hearing was still governed by the prior rules from when it was initially set in September 2024.

Ms. Corman argued that plaintiff's opposition improperly raised new issues not pleaded in the complaint. She contended that this tactic circumvented the pleading standards and was potentially reversible error. Ms. Corman requested the Court rule on evidentiary objections submitted with defendant's reply. The Court indicated that it did not see those objections in the briefing and, based on the arguments presented, determined that none of the objections were determinative of the motion and therefore declined to rule on them.

After hearing argument, the court adopted its tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT'S AMENDED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT is DENIED. The Court will consider Plaintiff's Opposition, given the hearing was initially set for September 2024 and continued multiple times. Plaintiff's evidence raises questions about Defendant's credibility, thereby raising triable issues of fact.

MOTION FOR LEAVE TO File An Amended Answer
Off Calendar Jul-18-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT FRANK LUCCHETTI INDIVIDUALLY AND D/B/A LUCCHETTI PROPERTIES's Motion for Leave to File An Amended Answer.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Matter is granted. The amended Answer must be filed and served not later than 25 July, 2024.

Amendments to the pleadings are very liberally granted, especially when there's no showing of prejudice to the other side. No prejudice to the plaintiff has been shown. The Opposition is based on the argument that the defense to be added has no merit, with plaintiff arguing (i) there are different burdens of proof, (ii) the non-availability of witnesses at an earlier hearing, and (iii) the fact that defendant has created a harassing living situation for the plaintiff. Oppos. at 5:6-6:24. These are issues of fact and law that are not easily susceptible to this sort of motion. A response on the merits of the defense might implicate (a) fact issues, which cannot now be resolved and (b) new arguments (on the law) to which courts normally will not attend when raised for the first time in reply.

The Opposition also notes that it doesn't matter whether this motion to amend is granted or not, as the issue of collateral estoppel can be raised in any event, id. at 8:13 ff., which begs the question why the opposition was filed at all. If the plaintiff nevertheless contends that as a matter of law the new affirmative defense should be deleted, plaintiff may e.g., demurrer to the answer. CCP section 430.50 (b).

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare and submit a proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/CK)
